full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: Like you said, I grew up in motorsport. My grandfather was a Silicon Valley guy, he had a great exit, he was the CEO of a company called JDS Uniphase and when he left he decided to then go on and invest in motorsport and automotive, so it's kind of in the genes. I would spend my summers when I was in college out in Indianapolis working for the team. And so kind of understood how teams operated. One of my jobs was taping up the car before we put it out on track. It's kind of one of those last half a percent improvements that you're always looking for when you're in a motorsport team, right? We take away some of the drag on the chassis by taping up around where the bodywork comes together. It also protects the chassis because out on long runs, you've got stuff flying at it, the atmosphere is changing all the time. So, you know, fast forward 10 years, we've just sold a business in the automotive technology space and I'm thinking about what I wanted to do to kind of bring my experiences as an investor and looking for technology companies that are developing proprietary data in the real world. And so I'm thinking how do we integrate that tape with the ability to generate the data in the real world. So, that's where we've got to. We've spent about two years building the hardware to get to the point. There it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: Um, yes, so the tape itself, what we generate is pressure, temperature and vibration at 500 Hertz. Uh, we can have up to 100 sensors on the vehicle at a time. Um, so between the data that we're generating off the tape and the metadata that we're collecting in terms of GPS and IMU data, so where the car is and how it is interacting, pitch, roll, yaw, acceleration, we're able to really start to generate a picture of kind of a digital twin in, in the atmosphere, in the real world. So why is that important? So this is, this is a car running in a wind tunnel. As you can see, um, the data is very binary, right? The, the car sits on the, on the treadmill, it starts spinning, the wind builds, we have fairly consistent pressure outside of a few turns of the turntable. That's probably simulating about two degrees of rotational turn. Um, and that's all you can really kind of demonstrate. Um, it allows you to look at very...

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: Specific things, but there's no way to really be able to understand how that's going to operate in the real world. Um, so if we bring up the real world data set, what we have here is a car operating on a real track, this is actually in your backyard, well not quite, but it's in Texas, um, with a well-known driver sets some recent success... and what we can see here is how the vehicle's actually operating in the real world. So we can see as they're accelerating, as they're braking, we're seeing different air speeds over the vehicle, and we're able to even look at you know areas where there's like stalls across the vehicle, um and in the future as we start to run in traffic, that will really allow us to understand, you know not just how the vehicle operates in a very sterile environment, in a simulated environment, but how it actually has to run in the real world where there's traffic, where there's changing conditions, everything else. I think, um, you know I don't know if anyone's seen the F1 movie, but we solve a lot of the questions that Brad Pitt wanted answered when he was was flirting with the engineer in the wind tunnels.

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: If you look at the F1 budget, right? They're capped at around 135, let's call it 135 for argument's sake. Based on my research, it's about a third of their budget goes towards aerodynamics. They have large teams in aerodynamics, they've got between 50 and 100 aerodynamicists, and they're using tools like wind tunnels and CFD, which are very limited by Formula 1 because over the last 20 years they've tried to keep the cost down. Now, in the good old days and cigarette sponsorship days, you used to be able to run separate engines for qualifying, for the race. There was no cap on these things and so spending got out of control. Now it's much more restrictive. The number one way to punish a team is to take its wind tunnel time away. So, you know, from that perspective, we came at this as an opportunity to significantly reduce the cost, but also to build a tool that people cannot get out of bed without thinking about how they're going to leverage AirShaper. So, and I know that you've had some views on this in the past, Jason, but I want to make this tape as...

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport

And they'll be paying ARR based on the functionality, based on the team, how many seats, how actively they're using that, and it'll be enterprise, you know, style ARR. But the tape itself, we still believe we can generate strong margins—without going into exactly what they are—but strong margins with a focal point laid out, 100 sensors for around $2,000. That gives you five hours of wind tunnel time, or five hours of data. If you compare that to a wind tunnel, we are in the order of 95% cheaper than the operating cost of turning that fan on and getting the data out of the wind tunnel, and it's on a much greater scale.

But now that we've locked in the architecture of our tape, we're really starting to look at how we ramp up the production to be able to satisfy a lot of uh, that demand. We are starting in motorsport because it's a market that we know very well, and we can very quickly prove every weekend somewhere in the US there is a team out there running, generating data, and using our product to improve their performance.

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: At 420 million data points an hour with 100 sensors on the car, we'll be able to grow that data set significantly over the next 12 months. But defense certainly makes sense, not just to make the vehicles go faster, but how do they go further? How do we reduce the drag to make them more efficient? Allow drone companies to really start to look at it from a more scientific standpoint as opposed to more of a commodity get them out there for cheap kind of way of thinking about things. full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I mean, one thing that I think is interesting, you're collecting so much data which is inherently really valuable, like how cars move through different scenarios or whatever. But you're collecting it from F1 teams who have got to be, you know, they're super protective of their designs, their engineering. So, like, how are you managing this data and are you allowed to hang on to the data that you collect from these, you know, from McLaren or Cadillac or a company like that?

full_transcript.speaker: Lyall Davenport
full_transcript.text: I think candidly we're going to get to a point where we're going to be like the frontier models in terms of some of them like they openly collect all of your data. You can pay more to have your data siloed, excluded from the sample set. You don't benefit from the rest of the sample set in the same way, but you know I think that there certainly will be teams especially at the higher end, you know, say the McLarens of the world that say to us, look, it's really important to us that our data is siloed and you don't use that to train the model. We can totally respect that, we can set up their own data lakes and infrastructure and everything else. For the large majority of the customers, they're just looking to how do we improve either the setup or the design of our vehicle in a way that actually allows us to understand how it's moving through the real world. And those are the customers where they'll be able to access this at a much more affordable level and we can then use it to make our model smarter so we're helping them in return as they're using the vertical model to think about design, setup, controls in the future as well. So how do we, how do we take the data off the vehicle, use onboard compute to then send instruction to the actuator so we can improve the efficiency of the vehicle either in flight or as it's driving?

highlights: Skim Systems is revolutionizing aerodynamics testing by replacing expensive, sterile wind tunnels with sensor-embedded tape that collects high-frequency data from vehicles in real-world conditions. This technology offers a 95% reduction in operating costs compared to traditional methods while providing "digital twins" for industries ranging from Formula 1 to defense and wind energy.

The U.S. government has reportedly intervened in the release of OpenAI’s newest frontier models after safety evaluations revealed the systems could exhibit "undesirable propensities" such as cheating and concealing misbehavior. This highlights a growing tension between rapid AI innovation and the necessity for national security backstops against advanced cyber threats.

Apple has implemented hardware price hikes of up to 25% on MacBooks and iPads, citing unprecedented surges in the cost of memory and storage components. This trend is largely driven by the massive infrastructure demands of AI data centers, which are increasingly viewed as a symbol of the economic gap between tech corporations and localized communities.

Meta is reportedly developing "Arena," a points-based prediction market app designed to engage the 18-34 demographic without the legal hurdles of real-money gambling. By gamifying the social conversation around predicting future events, the platform seeks to capitalize on the popularity of markets like PolyMarket while maintaining a safer regulatory profile.

The integration of Starlink into commercial aviation is expected to drastically alter travel preferences, as business travelers demonstrate a high willingness to pay premiums for fiber-like connectivity. Experts predict that the availability of reliable, high-speed internet will lead travelers to prioritize specific flight routes and airlines, such as United, over traditional cabin luxuries.
summary:
In this episode of *This Week in Startups*, Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris host Lyall Davenport, founder of Skim Systems, to discuss his company’s revolutionary approach to aerodynamics. By using specialized sensor-integrated tape to collect real-world data on vehicles, Skim Systems provides a significantly cheaper and more accurate alternative to traditional wind tunnel testing. While the company is currently focused on Formula 1 racing, the discussion explores the broader implications for defense, aviation, and infrastructure, highlighting a growing trend where hardware serves as a strategic "moat" in an increasingly AI-dominated software market.

The news docket focuses heavily on the intersection of big tech, government policy, and market shifts. The hosts analyze the U.S. government’s intervention in OpenAI’s model releases, debating whether these delays are necessary security precautions or a form of innovation-stifling censorship. They also cover Apple’s recent 20% price hikes and the loss of its top Vision Pro executive to OpenAI, signaling a growing rivalry as OpenAI moves into proprietary hardware. Further discussions include Mark Cuban’s warning that AI companies must "buy off" local communities to mitigate the backlash against noisy, energy-hungry data centers, and Mark Zuckerberg’s plan to launch a gamified, point-based prediction market at Meta.

The episode concludes with a somber tribute to the late legendary tech journalist Om Malik, emphasizing his integrity and profound influence on the industry. Throughout the "off-duty" segment, the hosts touch on themes of modern productivity and parenting, debating the high value of Starlink’s high-speed internet on flights and the market need for single-use consumer electronics—such as dedicated music players—to help families minimize the "doom scrolling" associated with smartphones.

youtube_metadata.title: Why F1 Teams are Replacing Wind Tunnels with Smart Tape | E2305
youtube_metadata.description:
This Week In Startups is made possible by:
Plaud - https://Plaud.ai/twist
Sentry - https://sentry.io/twist
Northwest Registered Agent-https://northwestregisteredagent.com/twist

DigitalOcean - https://do.co/twist

Today’s show:
F1 teams spend up to a third of their budgets on aerodynamics, often utilizing wind tunnels to simulate what happens on the track.
But Lyall Davenport of SKN Systems created a sensor-embedded tape that sticks directly to race cars as they drive around the real world, generating 420M data points per hour. And it comes in at roughly 95% less cost than running a full simulator.
Hear about how Lyall’s background in racing helped him develop the concept, plans to expand from motorsports to drones and defense, and his experiences going through Jason’s various programs.
PLUS OpenAI’s new models are so good, you can’t use them. Why Apple’s Vision Pro chief defected to OpenAI (and why their laptops and devices are getting more expensive). A look at the world’s most annoying data center in Sterling, Virginia. And Jason remembers iconic and influential tech journalist Om Malik.
